text: The surgeon introduces a Veress needle into the inferior crease of the umbilicus, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm proper placement.

The surgeon attaches the tubing of the insufflator to initiate pneumoperitoneum, with the preset pressure set to fifteen. The actual pressure gradually increases, starting with a flow rate of one liter per minute, then increasing to three liters per minute. Once pneumoperitoneum is achieved, the incision is enlarged to eleven millimeters.

The surgeon makes a smiling incision over the crease of the umbilicus and introduces the first optical trocar, accompanied by a hissing sound. The telescope is inserted, providing a view of the appendix, with all parameters including ETCO being normal.
The base of the appendix appears healthy, while the tip is visibly inflamed and adhered. The appendix is retrocecal and subserous, with adhesions to the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon prepares to perform an anti-grade appendectomy due to the appendix tip being adhered, inflamed, and at risk of rupture. Extra corporeal Misra's knot is used to secure the appendix.
The surgeon encounters and performs adhesiolysis on minor adhesions between the appendix and the small intestine, as well as the area near the cecum.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum covering part of the appendix using a surgical instrument. A Stryker mini alligator is used to examine the base of the appendix, followed by further peritoneal dissection to mobilize the appendix.

The tip of the appendix is visibly inflamed and adhered to the cecum. The surgeon prepares to use an extracorporeal knot to secure the structure, starting from the base of the appendix to avoid perforation while attempting to separate the tip.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the appendix and uses a harmonic scalpel with a Teflon jaw to carefully create a small window at the base of the appendix. The dissection is performed from both sides to avoid perforation, ensuring the cecum is not injured during the process.

The surgeon completes the blunt dissection through the buttonhole at the base of the appendix, using the harmonic device to open the jaw and finalize the window. A Vicryl number one suture is fed through the buttonhole for an extracorporeal knot to secure the continuous structure.

The surgeon feeds the suture through the buttonhole and applies a Misra knot at the base of the appendix. The knot is secured with a knot pusher, providing traction to effectively ligate the base of the appendix.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the appendix, leaving a five-millimeter margin towards the cecum. The energy from the harmonic seals the distal end, preventing fecolith slippage. The base of the appendix is separated and ligated, and the surgeon carefully dissects the appendix from the subserous area of the cecum, addressing the retrocecal and adhered nature of the appendix.

With one end of the appendix free, the surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, along with energy devices, to carefully dissect towards the inflamed tip of the appendix. Anterior traction is applied using a grasper, while managing minor oozing without rushing to stop it, to avoid injury to the cecum.

The surgeon carefully maintains a safe distance from the cecum while using a stripping technique to separate the inflamed and adhered appendix. Coagulation is applied, and the appendix is stripped down towards its tip. The tip area is adhered, and the fat is resistant to separation. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to sacrifice part of the tip, cutting it directly to avoid perforating the ascending colon or cecum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate the appendix from the ascending colon and cecum, ensuring the Teflon jaw is positioned towards the cecum and the vibrating jaw is directed towards the surgeon. The entire appendix is now visibly separated.
The surgeon uses a cloth to grasp the base of the appendix, pulling the entire appendix into the cannula for removal.

The surgeon uses an endo bag to contain the appendix within the cannula, followed by irrigation with betadine and suction to remove irritants. The patient is tilted to the right side for the flat tire test, where the ascending colon and cecum are submerged in saline and pressed to check for bubbles, indicating potential perforation.
